skill.name: neon-postgres
skill.description: Guides agents through Neon Serverless Postgres setup, connection choices, local development, branching, auth, APIs, SDKs, and operational features using official Neon documentation.
skill.summary: Use official Neon guidance to design, connect, and operate serverless Postgres projects.
skill.author: neondatabase

security_audit.summary: Static findings were reviewed against SKILL.md and no malicious behavior was found. The high weak-crypto and reconnaissance alerts are false positives from Markdown documentation text, while the network and command references support legitimate Neon documentation and CLI workflows.

low_findings.title: Official Documentation Network References
low_findings.description: The skill contains many hardcoded neon.com documentation URLs. These are intentional references to official Neon docs, not evidence of credential exfiltration or covert network behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All reviewed network references point to neon.com documentation pages. No evidence found of unrelated domains, secret collection, or data exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Legitimate CLI And Curl Guidance
low_findings.description: The static command findings are mostly Markdown code spans and documentation examples. The real command references describe fetching docs or using Neon CLI tools for expected database workflows.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command text is tied to official Neon documentation and Neon CLI use. It may still trigger external processes, so execution should remain user-approved.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptographic algorithm findings occur in descriptive Markdown and URL text. No cryptographic implementation or insecure hashing behavior is present in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains guidance text only and no crypto code. The matched lines do not define or call weak algorithms.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alert Is A False Positive
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance finding refers to a Neon connection pooling instruction about pooler hostnames. It does not collect local host data or instruct broad environment discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line describes a documented database hostname convention. No evidence found of system probing, inventory collection, or local reconnaissance.

content.value_statement: Teams need current guidance when they choose database drivers, Neon features, and deployment patterns. This skill directs Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to official Neon docs for practical Postgres decisions.
actual_capabilities: Finds official Neon documentation pages for common setup and operations topics.
Guides selection of TCP, HTTP, WebSocket, edge, and serverless connection methods.
Explains Neon features such as branching, autoscaling, scale to zero, restore, and read replicas.
Supports Neon Auth, Neon JS SDK, serverless driver, REST API, TypeScript SDK, and Python SDK workflows.
Points agents toward Neon CLI, MCP server, and local development setup guidance.
Helps plan pooling, IP allow lists, logical replication, and CI automation patterns.
limitations: It is a documentation guide and does not include executable automation scripts.
It depends on current Neon documentation for final accuracy.
It cannot access private Neon projects without user-provided credentials and approved tools.
It does not replace database design, performance testing, or security review for production systems.

use_cases.title: Choose A Connection Strategy
use_cases.description: Compare Neon connection methods for a web app, edge function, serverless function, or long-running service.

use_cases.title: Plan Preview Databases
use_cases.description: Use Neon branching guidance to design isolated environments for migrations, pull requests, and test data.

use_cases.title: Integrate Auth And Data APIs
use_cases.description: Review Neon Auth and Neon JS SDK guidance before adding managed users and PostgREST-style access.

prompt_templates.title: Start A Neon Project
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me set up a new Neon Serverless Postgres project for a web application. Include driver choice, connection handling, and first schema steps.

prompt_templates.title: Pick The Right Driver
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare Neon connection methods for my runtime and explain when to use TCP, HTTP, WebSocket, pooling, or the serverless driver.

prompt_templates.title: Design Branching Workflows
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Neon branching workflow for pull request previews, schema migration testing, and cleanup after deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Automate Neon Operations
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan an automation workflow using Neon CLI, the REST API, or SDKs for branch creation, endpoint management, polling, and CI integration.

output_examples.input: Which Neon connection method should I use for a Next.js app on serverless infrastructure?
output: A short comparison of pooled Postgres, HTTP queries, and WebSocket transactions.
A recommendation based on runtime limits, concurrency, and transaction needs.
Links to the relevant official Neon documentation pages.

output_examples.input: How should my team use Neon branches for pull request previews?
output: A branch lifecycle plan covering creation, migration, preview deployment, review, and cleanup.
Operational notes about endpoints, copy-on-write behavior, and cost controls.
A list of checks to run before merging database changes.

output_examples.input: I need to add Neon Auth and query user data from my app.
output: A guided explanation of Neon Auth, Neon JS SDK, and PostgREST-style querying options.
Setup considerations for React or Next.js applications.
Warnings about credentials, client boundaries, and production review.

best_practices: Verify feature details against official Neon documentation before final implementation.
Ask for the target runtime, framework, and transaction needs before recommending a driver.
Require user approval before running CLI commands or accessing project-specific resources.
anti_patterns: Do not guess Neon documentation URLs when the docs index should be checked.
Do not expose database credentials in prompts, logs, client code, or examples.
Do not recommend one connection method without considering runtime constraints and pooling needs.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps agents answer Neon Serverless Postgres questions using official setup, driver, feature, API, and operations guidance.

faq.question: Does it run Neon commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and references. Any CLI or API action should require explicit user approval.

faq.question: Can it help choose a Postgres driver?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers TCP, HTTP, WebSocket, edge, serverless, pooled, and long-running connection patterns.

faq.question: Can it support production database planning?
faq.answer: It can guide planning, but production systems still need testing, access review, backup planning, and security review.

faq.question: Does it cover Neon Auth?
faq.answer: Yes. It points agents to Neon Auth and Neon JS SDK guidance for managed users and data access workflows.

faq.question: Why does the audit show network risk factors?
faq.answer: The skill links to official Neon documentation and suggests fetching current docs, which is legitimate but still uses network access.
